skill.name: gws-calendar
skill.description: Use the Google Workspace CLI to inspect and manage Google Calendar calendars, events, ACLs, free/busy data, settings, and watches.
skill.summary: Manage Google Calendar resources through guided gws CLI commands.
skill.author: googleworkspace

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many Ruby backtick execution hits, path traversal hits, and weak cryptography blockers. Review found the backticks are Markdown inline code or fenced bash examples, the path traversal hits are relative documentation links, and no prompt injection or malicious exfiltration text was found in SKILL.md. The skill still has medium risk because it instructs use of the gws CLI for powerful Google Calendar operations, including delete, clear, ACL updates, and watch methods.

medium_findings.title: Powerful Calendar Mutation Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill documents gws Calendar methods that can delete events, clear a primary calendar, delete secondary calendars, and change ACL rules. These are legitimate administration functions, but an agent should require explicit user confirmation before running destructive or permission-changing operations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented methods directly include delete, clear, update, patch, and ACL operations. The behavior is legitimate for the skill purpose, but the impact on calendar data and access controls is clear.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution Required
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to call the gws CLI and inspect schemas before invoking Calendar API methods. This is expected for a Google Workspace CLI skill, but it depends on local authentication state and can act on real account data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and semantically required by the skill. There is no command injection evidence, but executing the CLI can affect authenticated Google Calendar resources.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged many backtick-delimited method names and command examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. In context, these are Markdown formatting and fenced bash examples, not executable Ruby code embedded in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown documentation, and the flagged text appears inside inline code spans or fenced code blocks. No Ruby file or Ruby execution construct is present.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Links Misclassified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The ../ sequences point to sibling skill documentation for shared instructions and helper commands. They do not read arbitrary paths by themselves, although consumers should avoid following untrusted links outside the skill package without review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relative paths occur in Markdown links and a prerequisite note, not file access code. The links are bounded to adjacent documented skill files in the repository layout.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Match Not Supported
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography blockers at the description and command table lines are not supported by the reviewed content. No hash algorithm, encryption API, or credential handling logic appears at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is the plain skill description and line 24 is a Markdown table header. I did not find evidence of cryptographic code or weak algorithm usage in those locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Calendar API Methods Documented
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill exposes commands for deleting ACL rules, clearing calendars, deleting calendars, and deleting events. These methods should be treated as dangerous operations that need confirmation and narrow parameters.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous operations are explicitly named in the method list. The risk is operational impact, not evidence of malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Calendar Watch Methods May Create Persistent Change Notifications
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents watch methods for ACL, CalendarList, Events, and Settings resources. Watch operations are legitimate, but they can create ongoing notification channels and should be scoped and reviewed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The watch methods are directly listed for several resources. No endpoint or exfiltration behavior is shown, so the concern is persistence and scope control.

content.value_statement: Calendar work often requires precise commands across events, ACLs, free/busy checks, and settings. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to inspect schemas and run Google Workspace CLI calendar commands with clear parameters.
actual_capabilities: Lists available Google Calendar API resources and methods exposed by the gws calendar command.
Guides schema inspection before building calendar method parameters and JSON payloads.
Supports event operations such as list, get, insert, patch, update, move, delete, and instances.
Supports calendar, calendarList, ACL, colors, freebusy, settings, and channel operations.
Points users to helper commands for event insertion and agenda views when available.
Documents watch methods for Calendar resources that support change notifications.
limitations: Requires the gws CLI and valid Google Workspace authentication outside the skill.
Does not provide built-in confirmation safeguards for destructive methods.
Does not include detailed examples for every Calendar API method.
Depends on separate shared instructions for authentication, global flags, and security rules.

use_cases.title: Schedule and maintain team events
use_cases.description: Create, update, move, or delete Google Calendar events after inspecting the required schema.

use_cases.title: Audit calendar access controls
use_cases.description: Review, add, patch, update, or delete ACL rules for calendars with explicit scope.

use_cases.title: Check availability before planning
use_cases.description: Query free/busy information and list calendar events before choosing meeting windows.

prompt_templates.title: List calendar resources
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the available gws calendar resources and methods. Do not change any calendar data.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect an event method
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the schema for listing Google Calendar events, then explain the required parameters before running anything.

prompt_templates.title: Create a calendar event
prompt_templates.prompt: Use gws calendar to create an event on my primary calendar. Confirm the title, time, attendees, and calendar ID first.

prompt_templates.title: Review ACL changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the ACL update schema, summarize the exact permission change, and wait for approval before applying it.

output_examples.input: Find the right command to list events on my primary calendar.
output_examples.output: The assistant inspects the events list schema, identifies calendar ID and time range parameters, and proposes a read-only command.

output_examples.input: Create a planning meeting next Tuesday with three attendees.
output_examples.output: The assistant asks for missing time zone and calendar details, confirms the event fields, then prepares the gws calendar insert command.

output_examples.input: Remove an old shared calendar from my list.
output_examples.output: The assistant distinguishes removing a calendar from the user list versus deleting a calendar, then requests confirmation before any change.

best_practices: Inspect the method schema before constructing parameters or JSON payloads.
Use read-only methods first when verifying calendar IDs, event IDs, or ACL rule IDs.
Require explicit confirmation before delete, clear, update, patch, move, or ACL operations.
anti_patterns: Running destructive calendar commands without repeating the target calendar and event details.
Using a service account as the unintended owner for newly created calendars.
Creating watch channels without documenting their scope, destination, and expiration.

faq.question: Does this skill authenticate to Google Calendar by itself?
faq.answer: No. It depends on the installed gws CLI and the user's existing Google Workspace authentication.

faq.question: Can it create and update calendar events?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents event insert, patch, update, move, quickAdd, instances, list, get, and delete methods.

faq.question: Can it manage calendar permissions?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents ACL get, list, insert, patch, update, delete, and watch methods.

faq.question: Is it safe for destructive actions?
faq.answer: It can guide destructive methods, so users should require confirmation and exact target IDs before changes.

faq.question: Does it support free/busy checks?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes the freebusy query resource for checking availability across calendars.

faq.question: Why does the audit show medium risk?
faq.answer: The skill uses an external CLI with real Google Calendar access and exposes delete, clear, ACL, and watch operations.
